title: Delete the Middle Node of a Linked List
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: C++ proposal for this challenge: the recommended fast+lean solution is also the fastest-runtime proposal, so no separate solution-runtime.cpp is shipped. The minimum-memory champion is implemented in C; see crossReferences.

variants.languageReason: The workload is a single pointer-heavy traversal over up to 100000 linked-list nodes. C++ gives native pointer rewiring, direct compatibility with LeetCode's ListNode* class signature, no GC/interpreter overhead, and O(1) auxiliary memory. The same implementation is also the fastest-runtime proposal because it reaches the singly linked-list traversal lower bound in one pass.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected for the recommended and runtime objectives: direct ListNode* mutation, native branch-light loops, no auxiliary allocation, and the standard LeetCode class submission surface.
Considered and used for the minimum-memory proposal. It can reduce reported runtime baseline memory, but it does not improve the one-pass runtime algorithm over C++.
Considered for native performance and safety; LeetCode's platform-owned linked-list representation adds ownership ceremony without reducing time or auxiliary memory.
Considered for compiled pointer traversal; runtime and GC metadata are unnecessary for this O(1)-state linked-list operation.
Considered for strong JIT performance; managed object layout, runtime baseline, and null-check overhead are less attractive for top-percentile pointer traversal.
Considered for clarity; interpreter/VM overhead loses to native code for up to 100000 node visits.

variants.approachJustification: Use fast/slow pointers to place slow at index floor(n / 2), retain the predecessor as prev, and unlink the middle node with one pointer assignment.

reasoningSummary: Fast/slow pointers derive the floor(n / 2) middle during one traversal. Tracking the previous slow pointer provides the predecessor needed for singly linked-list deletion, so the middle is removed with one pointer rewrite. The C++ solution is both the recommended and runtime-optimal proposal; the C sibling targets lower reported memory baseline.
